ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chicken sausage with skin; SNOMED CT coverage for processed poultry products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken sausage with skin is one sausage link weighing approximately 60 g, providing around 109 kcal, 10 g protein, and 6.9 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken sausage, with skin — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chicken sausage with skin (record ID F06110007). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken sausage with skin in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on the closest analogous processed meat product (sausage, meat-based) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Carbohydrate content is low at 3.05 g/100g; glycemic load per 60 g serving = (28 × 1.83) / 100 = 0.51, rounded to 0.5. Low GI is consistent with the high protein and fat content suppressing glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1542 mg/100g — more than 2.5× the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chicken sausage with skin; even a 60 g serving delivers approximately 925 mg sodium, representing over 40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB, and regular consumption is strongly associated with elevated blood pressure and increased cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.05 g/100g) and high protein (16.66 g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but very high sodium and processed-meat classification warrant caution
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken sausage with skin has a low glycaemic index and low carbohydrate content that minimally raises blood glucose, making it conditionally acceptable for people with type 2 diabetes in small portions; however, the very high sodium content (1542 mg/100g) may worsen insulin resistance over time and the processed-meat classification is associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk in epidemiological studies, so frequency should be limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (1542 mg/100g), saturated fat (~3.2 g/100g), and processed-meat classification with likely sodium nitrite preservative use
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of chicken sausage with skin; the combination of very high sodium, saturated fat, and processed-meat preservatives (sodium nitrite) is associated with elevated blood pressure, endothelial dysfunction, and increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1542 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; sodium nitrite in processed meats is associated with increased systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chicken sausage with skin; the very high sodium content worsens joint and tissue swelling, and sodium nitrite used as a preservative in processed meats is associated with elevated inflammatory markers and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken sausage with skin is a processed poultry product made from minced chicken meat and skin that is seasoned, mixed with salt and spices, and encased in a sausage casing. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and hawker-adjacent retail settings and is commonly consumed at breakfast, as a snack, or as part of a cooked meal. The texture is firm and slightly springy, with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour derived from the seasoning blend and the natural fat content of the chicken skin. Per 100 g, it provides 181 kcal, 16.66 g protein, 11.43 g fat, and only 3.05 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. However, the sodium content of 1542 mg/100g is extremely high — more than 2.5 times the 600 mg/100g threshold considered safe for hypertension-sensitive individuals — and classifies this food as a high-sodium-warning product. The glycaemic index is estimated as low (~28), consistent with its minimal carbohydrate content and high protein-fat matrix. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium load, and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by people with type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis. It is labelled halal and is suitable for keto dietary patterns due to its low carbohydrate content, but the high sodium and processed-meat classification limit its overall nutritional quality.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in chicken sausage?
Is chicken sausage good for weight loss?
Can I eat chicken sausage if I have high blood pressure?
Chicken sausage vs pork sausage nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F06110007) at confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific chicken sausage with skin entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06110007) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken sausage (NDB 07072) as a secondary reference, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this record; this is documented as an adapted value. GI value of 28 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for processed meat sausage; no direct GI measurement for chicken sausage with skin exists in the approved GI database. Dietary tag 'keto' retained from input as carbohydrate is 3.05 g/100g, consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds. Sodium nitrite is commonly used as a preservative in processed chicken sausages; this is noted in health context guidance but is not confirmed for all brands.
